abstract: Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) have achieved strong performance on many video understanding tasks, but most existing systems remain offline and are not well-suited for live video streams that require continuous observation and timely response. Recent streaming VideoLLMs have made progress, yet current approaches often rely on decoupled trigger-response pipelines or are limited to captioning-style narration, reducing their effectiveness for open-ended question answering and long-horizon interaction. We propose AURA (Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance), an end-to-end streaming visual interaction framework that enables a unified VideoLLM to continuously process video streams and support both real-time question answering and proactive responses. AURA integrates context management, data construction, training objectives, and deployment optimization for stable long-horizon streaming interaction. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on streaming benchmarks and supports a real-time demo system with ASR and TTS running at 2 FPS on two 80G accelerators. We release the AURA model together with a real-time inference framework to facilitate future research.

citations.fullCitation: Junming Lin, Zheng Fang, Chi Chen, Zihao Wan, Fuwen Luo, Peng Li, Yang Liu, and Maosong Sun. StreamingBench: Assessing the gap for mllms to achieve streaming video understanding. arXiv preprint arXiv:2411.03628, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper introduces StreamingBench, one of the primary benchmarks used to evaluate AURA. The authors use this benchmark extensively to demonstrate AURA's state-of-the-art performance in real-time visual understanding and contextual reasoning over continuous video streams, making it central to validating the paper's claims.
citations.title: StreamingBench: Assessing the gap for mllms to achieve streaming video understanding

citations.fullCitation: Junbo Niu, Yifei Li, Ziyang Miao, Chunjiang Ge, Yuanhang Zhou, Qihao He, Xiaoyi Dong, Haodong Duan, Shuangrui Ding, Rui Qian, et al. OVO-Bench: How far is your video-llms from real-world online video understanding? In Proc. CVPR, pages 18902–18913, 2025.
citations.justification: OVO-Bench is a crucial benchmark cited and used in the paper to evaluate AURA's capabilities in online video understanding, particularly its temporal awareness. The AURA paper presents its strong results on this benchmark as evidence of its proficiency in backward tracing, real-time perception, and proactive response scenarios.
citations.title: OVO-Bench: How far is your video-llms from real-world online video understanding?

citations.fullCitation: Joya Chen, Zhaoyang Lv, Shiwei Wu, Kevin Qinghong Lin, Chenan Song, Difei Gao, Jia-Wei Liu, Ziteng Gao, Dongxing Mao, and Mike Zheng Shou. VideoLLM-Online: Online video large language model for streaming video. In Proc. CVPR, pages 18407–18418, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper presents a foundational unified framework for streaming video dialogue, which is directly related to AURA's architectural approach. The AURA paper builds upon this paradigm, positioning itself as an advancement that addresses the challenges of robustness and long-duration inference that were limitations in prior unified models.
citations.title: VideoLLM-Online: Online video large language model for streaming video

citations.fullCitation: Rui Qian, Shuangrui Ding, Xiaoyi Dong, Pan Zhang, Yuhang Zang, Yuhang Cao, Dahua Lin, and Jiaqi Wang. Dispider: Enabling video llms with active real-time interaction via disentangled perception, decision, and reaction. In Proc. CVPR, pages 24045–24055, 2025.
citations.justification: The AURA paper cites Dispider as a key example of a 'decoupled' architecture for streaming video, which separates perception and reaction modules. This is important because AURA positions its own 'unified' architecture in direct contrast, arguing that an end-to-end approach provides more consistent and stable system behavior.
citations.title: Dispider: Enabling video llms with active real-time interaction via disentangled perception, decision, and reaction

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the technical paper "AURA: Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance via Video Streams." It covers the authors, the work's context within the broader research landscape, key objectives, methodology, main findings, and its significance and potential impact.

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team from Huawei Research and CUHK MMLab. The authors are Xudong Lu, Yang Bo, Jinpeng Chen, Shuhan Li, Xintong Guo, Huankang Guan, Fang Liu, Dunyuan Xu, Peiwen Sun, Heyang Sun, Rui Liu, and Hongsheng Li. Yang Bo, Jinpeng Chen, Shuhan Li, Xintong Guo, Huankang Guan, Fang Liu, Dunyuan Xu, Heyang Sun, and Rui Liu are affiliated with Huawei Research. Xudong Lu, Peiwen Sun, and Hongsheng Li are affiliated with CUHK MMLab. Xudong Lu, Yang Bo, Jinpeng Chen, Shuhan Li, Xintong Guo, and Huankang Guan are noted as having equal contributions. Yang Bo is identified as the Project Lead, while Rui Liu and Hongsheng Li are Corresponding Authors.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) represent a paradigm for video understanding, integrating visual perception with language-based reasoning. These models have demonstrated capabilities across tasks such as video captioning, video question answering, and temporal grounding, indicating their potential for applications in areas like public safety monitoring and intelligent transportation.

However, the majority of existing VideoLLMs are designed for offline processing. In this setup, a complete video or a buffered segment is analyzed retrospectively, which limits their utility in scenarios requiring real-time responsiveness, such as AI assistants, live narration, and interactive robotic systems. To address this, a subset of research has begun to explore streaming VideoLLMs, which continuously process incoming video frames, incrementally update understanding, and provide real-time or proactive responses.

Current streaming VideoLLMs generally fall into two categories: decoupled and unified architectures. Decoupled systems typically employ a lightweight trigger model separate from the main VideoLLM to decide when a response is needed. This approach can lead to inconsistencies due to the models not sharing contextual states and the smaller scale of trigger models. Unified architectures, while offering a higher performance potential, have often been limited to captioning-style narration or lack robustness for long-duration streaming interactions, potentially encountering issues such as memory overflow or performance degradation over extended inference periods. This work aims to develop a robust, end-to-end unified streaming VideoLLM framework that overcomes these limitations, enabling continuous observation, selective response, and stable long-horizon interaction.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The motivation for developing AURA stems from the identified limitations of existing VideoLLMs when applied to live video streams and real-time interaction scenarios. Current VideoLLMs are primarily designed for offline analysis, processing pre-buffered or complete video segments. This inherent latency makes them unsuitable for applications requiring immediate understanding and timely responses to ongoing events.

The paper identifies two main challenges with existing streaming VideoLLMs:
1.  **Decoupled vs. Limited Unified Architectures:** Decoupled streaming VideoLLMs, which use separate models for triggering responses and generating content, can suffer from inconsistent behavior because the trigger model and the primary VideoLLM do not share a unified contextual state. Unified architectures, while conceptually superior, often have restricted capabilities, predominantly supporting captioning-style narration rather than complex, open-ended question answering or long-horizon interactions. Furthermore, some unified models exhibit instability over extended streaming durations.
2.  **Unbounded Context Management:** Continuous video streams and user-model interactions inherently generate an unbounded context. Traditional Large Language Models (LLMs) have limited context windows, and managing this growing context efficiently while maintaining real-time performance is a significant challenge. An excessively long context can lead to increased inference time and compromised responsiveness.

To address these issues, the key objectives of the AURA framework are:
1.  **Enable Unified Continuous Processing with Selective Response:** To allow a single, unified VideoLLM to continuously process video streams frame by frame and autonomously decide whether to remain silent or generate an appropriate response, moving beyond a simple trigger-response mechanism.
2.  **Stably Handle Unbounded Video-Text Inputs:** To manage the continuously growing video and textual interaction history over extended durations without suffering from performance degradation or memory issues, while ensuring timely responses.
3.  **Support Diverse Interaction Modes:** To facilitate not only real-time question answering but also proactive responses and multi-response interactions, aligning with natural human-like conversational patterns in streaming contexts.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

AURA proposes an end-to-end streaming visual interaction framework, developed through the co-design of data, algorithms, and systems, to achieve stability, broad capabilities, and long-term endurance in streaming video understanding.

**4.1. Interactive Video Stream Context Management**
To manage the trade-off between unbounded input and limited LLM context, AURA introduces a context management mechanism:
*   **Chunk-wise Conversational Format:** Video streams are processed in small temporal chunks. For each chunk, a user message is formatted to include the video chunk and any user question issued concurrently. If no question is present, the message contains only the video. Each user message is followed by an assistant message, containing either a text response or a special `<|silent|>` token indicating no response.
*   **Dual Sliding-Window Strategy:** AURA employs a dual sliding-window strategy to manage context:
    *   **Video Window:** A fixed-length sliding window (e.g., *N*=30 seconds) is maintained for the most recent video content, as visual tokens are dense and recent content is often most relevant.
    *   **QA Interaction Window:** A separate sliding window is maintained for the most recent *M* QA groups (e.g., *M*=10), where a QA group includes a user question and all subsequent non-silent assistant responses. For QA groups falling outside the video window, only the textual content is preserved, discarding video chunks and silent tokens. This strategy balances retaining critical historical context with controlling total context length.

**4.2. Streaming QA Types**
Based on the context management, AURA defines three types of streaming QA interactions that reflect diverse response timing and frequency requirements:
*   **Real-Time QA:** The model generates a single, immediate response based on the current or previously observed visual context.
*   **Proactive QA:** The model remains silent initially, generating a single response only after sufficient future visual evidence has accumulated.
*   **Multi-Response QA:** The model continuously monitors events and produces multiple responses over time as new visual information becomes available, without requiring repeated queries.

**4.3. Coarse-to-Fine Streaming Data Engine**
A five-stage pipeline generates streaming training data for these QA types:
1.  **Video Preparation:** High-quality videos from diverse public internet sources are collected, resampled to 2 FPS, and re-encoded for consistency and stability.
2.  **QA Synthesis:**
    *   **Real-Time and Proactive QA Pipeline:** A multimodal large language model (MLLM) segments videos into scenes and generates scene descriptions. The MLLM then proposes candidate QA pairs with timestamps. For Real-Time QA, question and answer share a timestamp; for Proactive QA, the question precedes the answer. A verification step ensures questions are reasonable and answers are supported by video content up to the answer timestamp.
    *   **Multi-Response QA Pipeline:** An MLLM segments scenes with time intervals and generates candidate questions. Questions are verified for reasonableness and potential for multiple answers. The MLLM then generates multiple answers with timestamps, which are verified for accuracy and grounding.
3.  **QA Refinement:**
    *   **Real-Time QA:** Focuses on difficulty diversity. For each original QA, the MLLM generates four additional questions of progressively increasing difficulty at the same timestamp. One question is sampled, and an aligned answer is generated.
    *   **Proactive and Multi-Response QA:** Focuses on phrasing diversity. Questions are rewritten into semantically equivalent forms using predefined templates, preserving key entities and temporal references.
4.  **Streaming Structuring:** Timestamped QA annotations are converted into training samples matching the Interactive Video Stream Context Management. Each continuous QA sequence from a video is unrolled into multiple training samples. Each sample contains the interaction history up to one non-silent assistant message (the "target answer"), which serves as the supervision anchor. Earlier interactions are retained as contextual history.
5.  **Quality Verification:** This stage filters out unreliable samples. For Real-Time QA, it verifies that the target answer is visually grounded, factually correct, temporally consistent, and free of hallucination given the truncated context. For Proactive and Multi-Response QA, it checks for temporal appropriateness, accuracy, and grounding.
    *   **Additional Details:** Short acknowledgments are inserted for Proactive and Multi-Response QA. Samples from different QA types are combined into mixed interaction sequences to simulate realistic streaming sessions.

**4.4. Training and Inference Design**
*   **Silent-Speech Balanced Loss:** This objective addresses two challenges:
    *   **Supervision Selection:** Loss is applied only to all silent assistant messages and the *last* non-silent assistant message in each training sample. This prevents the model from being supervised by potentially ungrounded earlier responses after context truncation.
    *   **Silence/Speech Imbalance:** Since silent messages (`<|silent|>`) are far more frequent, a class-balancing strategy is used. Non-silent response tokens receive a weight of 1, while silent message tokens are down-weighted by the inverse of their frequency (1/*N*<sub>silent</sub>), preventing the model from over-predicting silence.
*   **Real-Time Streaming Inference Framework:** An end-to-end system integrates the AURA model with ASR and TTS modules, incorporating optimizations:
    *   **Context Construction:** Leverages the Interactive Video Stream Context Management. User speech is transcribed by ASR and combined with video chunks. AURA generates text responses, which are converted to speech by TTS.
    *   **Context Truncation Optimization:** To enable KV-cache reuse, the video window is allowed to extend slightly (e.g., *N'*=15 chunks beyond *N*). When the window reaches *N*+*N'*, *N'* oldest chunks are removed, and the KV cache for the remaining *N* chunks is recomputed. This limits recomputations. The text QA window is trimmed simultaneously with the video window.
    *   **Asynchronous Operation:** AURA, ASR, and TTS operate asynchronously to minimize latency.
    *   **Optimizations:** Includes streaming output, computation graph optimization, and multi-process resource isolation.

**4.5. Implementation Details**
AURA is built upon Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct. The LLM component is fine-tuned while the vision encoder and connector are frozen. Training data consists of approximately 115k streaming video QA samples (1.04B tokens) and 59k in-house offline video QA samples (0.16B tokens). Training involves one epoch with a global batch size of 128 and a learning rate of 1 × 10<sup>−5</sup>. Hyperparameters for context management are set to *N*=30 seconds, *N'*=15 chunks, and *M*=10 QA groups.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

**5.1. Performance on Streaming Benchmarks**
AURA was evaluated against proprietary models (GPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-Pro) and open-source baselines (StreamAgent, ViSpeak, Qwen3-VL-8B-Inst., MiniCPM-o-4.5, Streamo-7B, M4) on three streaming video understanding benchmarks: StreamingBench, OVO-Bench, and OmniMMI.

*   **StreamingBench:** AURA achieved an overall accuracy of 73.1%, outperforming the strongest open-source baseline (MiniCPM-o-4.5) by 10.4%. It also surpassed Gemini-1.5-Pro by 6.0%. AURA demonstrated broad superiority, ranking first among open-source models across all three high-level groups (Real-Time Visual Understanding, Omni-Source Understanding, Contextual Understanding) and on 14 of 18 fine-grained sub-tasks.
*   **OVO-Bench:** AURA obtained the highest overall accuracy of 65.3%, improving over the best open-source result (ViSpeak) by 4.2%. It outperformed Gemini-1.5-Pro by 2.3% overall and was the best open-source model on Real-Time Visual Perception and Backward Tracing.
*   **OmniMMI:** AURA achieved the best overall accuracy of 25.4%, surpassing all other models, including proprietary ones. It ranked first on 5 of 9 fine-grained metrics.

**5.2. Inference Performance**
*   **AURA Inference Performance:** An evaluation of AURA's inference engine, built on vLLM, demonstrated the effectiveness of its context management and cache reuse. AURA achieved the lowest average Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) compared to configurations without a sliding window or prefix caching. Disabling prefix caching led to consistently high TTFT, while disabling the sliding window resulted in TTFT increasing over time due to unbounded context growth. AURA maintained a bounded number of computed tokens, sustaining lower TTFT.
*   **End-to-End Latency:** The end-to-end system, integrating ASR (Qwen3-ASR-1.7B), AURA, and TTS (Qwen3-TTS-12Hz-1.7B-Base), achieved an estimated end-to-end latency from user speech input to the first spoken response of approximately 312.2 ms. This breakdown included:
    *   ASR: 84.2 ms for a 9.41-second audio input.
    *   AURA (server-side TTFT): 75.0 ms (average over 5 minutes of continuous streaming).
    *   First-sentence decode time: Approximately 60 ms (for 8 tokens at 7.3 ms/token).
    *   TTS first chunk latency: 93.0 ms (average, with a Real-Time Factor of 0.42).
    The model's generation speed was approximately 7.3 ms per token (≈137 tokens/s).

**5.3. Research Questions**
*   **RQ1: Accuracy on Offline Video Benchmarks:** AURA was compared to its base model, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct, on offline benchmarks (LongVideoBench, MVBench, Video-MME). AURA achieved 58.8% on LongVideoBench, 68.1% on MVBench, and 65.1% on Video-MME. While showing modest performance drops on LongVideoBench and Video-MME compared to the base model, it remained particularly close on MVBench. This suggests that streaming-oriented training largely preserves offline video understanding capabilities.
*   **RQ2: Effect of the Training Objective:** An ablation study on OmniMMI evaluated the Silent-Speech Balanced Loss against a default cross-entropy loss. Replacing the proposed objective with the default loss led to a substantial drop in overall accuracy (from 25.4% to 16.4%) and a complete failure in Proactive Alerting (PA) (from 37.5% to 0.0%). The variant trained with default loss exhibited a tendency to over-generate silent tokens and produced ungrounded responses for non-proactive interactions. This confirms the importance of the Silent-Speech Balanced Loss in balancing silent and speech predictions and mitigating hallucination risk due to context truncation.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The development of AURA represents an advancement in the field of streaming VideoLLMs, moving beyond the limitations of previous offline, decoupled, or restricted unified approaches. By presenting an end-to-end framework, AURA addresses critical challenges associated with continuous observation, selective silence, timely response, and long-horizon context management in live video streams.

The demonstrated performance of AURA on streaming benchmarks, where it consistently outperformed both open-source and, in many cases, proprietary models, suggests its capability for real-time visual understanding and interaction. The co-design of its data construction pipeline, training objectives, and efficient inference framework contributes to its stability and broad applicability. Specifically, the Interactive Video Stream Context Management offers a pragmatic solution for handling unbounded multimodal inputs, while the Coarse-to-Fine Streaming Data Engine and Silent-Speech Balanced Loss provide structured methods for training models to exhibit nuanced conversational behaviors, including proactive and multi-response interactions.

The achievement of a low end-to-end latency (approximately 312.2 ms) in the integrated ASR-AURA-TTS system, coupled with its real-time operational capability at 2 FPS on dual accelerators, signifies its practical usability for real-world applications. This performance indicates AURA's potential to power always-on visual assistants, interactive robotic systems, and other intelligent visual systems that require immediate and continuous contextual understanding. The findings also suggest that specialized streaming-oriented training can enhance online interaction abilities without substantially degrading performance on conventional offline video understanding tasks.

The release of the AURA model and its real-time inference framework aims to facilitate further research in streaming video understanding and accelerate the development of practical, interactive, and proactive visual assistance technologies.
overview:
## The Evolution of Video Understanding: From Offline to Streaming

Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) have significantly advanced the ability of AI to interpret visual content, enabling tasks like automated captioning, temporal grounding, and complex question-answering. Historically, these models operated in an offline mode: they processed a complete video file or a pre-buffered segment all at once. While effective for analyzing recorded content, this retrospective approach is ill-suited for real-world applications that require immediate feedback, such as live AI assistants, interactive robotics, and real-time security monitoring.

To move toward human-like interaction, research has shifted toward streaming VideoLLMs. These models continuously ingest video frames, update their understanding incrementally, and provide responses as events unfold. However, existing streaming systems often struggle with two major issues: architectural inconsistency and the management of unbounded information. Some systems use a "decoupled" approach, where a small model decides when to trigger a larger model to respond. This can lead to fragmented understanding because the two models do not share the same internal state. Others use "unified" architectures but are often limited to simple narrations and fail during long, open-ended conversations.

![Interactive streaming scenarios showing Real-Time, Proactive, and Multi-Response QA.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04184v1/x3.png)

AURA (Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance) addresses these limitations by introducing a robust, end-to-end unified framework. It is designed to process video streams frame by frame, autonomously deciding when to stay silent and when to speak. By co-designing data generation, context management algorithms, and the underlying system architecture, AURA maintains stable, long-term interactions without the memory or performance degradation typical of previous methods.

## Overcoming the Challenges of Unbounded Streams

Operating on a continuous live stream presents a fundamental problem: context growth. In a standard Large Language Model (LLM), the "context window" refers to the maximum amount of information the model can consider at once. In a live video stream, the amount of data (video frames and conversation history) grows indefinitely. If not managed, this unbounded context eventually exceeds the model’s memory limits or causes inference speed to drop, making real-time response impossible.

AURA introduces an Interactive Video Stream Context Management mechanism to solve this. Instead of treating the stream as one continuous block, it processes video in small temporal "chunks." For every chunk, the system constructs a user message that includes the video data and any concurrent user query. To enable a unified response pattern, the model outputs either a text response or a special `<|silent|>` token. This token allows the model to "observe" the stream without being forced to generate redundant or ungrounded text.

## Managing Context with Dual Sliding Windows

To ensure the model remains efficient over long durations, AURA employs a dual sliding-window strategy. This strategy recognizes that visual information and textual interaction history have different importance levels over time.

1.  **Video Window:** Because video tokens are dense and computationally expensive, the system maintains a fixed-length window for the most recent video content. For example, it might keep only the last $N$ seconds (e.g., $N=30$) of video.
2.  **QA Interaction Window:** Critical conversational context—like what a user asked two minutes ago—must often be preserved even if the corresponding video frames have been discarded. AURA maintains a separate window for the most recent $M$ QA groups (e.g., $M=10$). A QA group consists of a user question and the resulting non-silent responses.

![The Interactive Video Stream Context Management framework showing chunk-wise formatting and window truncation.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04184v1/x2.png)

This dual-window approach allows the model to balance the need for recent visual evidence with the need for long-term conversational memory. When data falls outside these windows, AURA discards the high-density video tokens but retains the textual summaries of past interactions, preventing memory overflow while preserving continuity.

## Streaming Interaction Patterns

Unlike offline models that respond to a single prompt, a streaming assistant must handle various timing requirements. AURA is trained to support three distinct interaction modes that mimic natural human behavior:

*   **Real-Time QA:** This is the most common mode, where the model provides an immediate answer to a user's question based on what it is currently seeing or has just seen.
*   **Proactive QA:** In some cases, a user might ask a question that cannot be answered immediately (e.g., "Tell me when the person finishes the task"). The model must remain silent for several seconds or minutes, observing the stream until the event occurs, and then proactively provide the answer.
*   **Multi-Response QA:** For continuous monitoring (e.g., "Keep me updated on the traffic"), the model generates multiple responses over time as new information arrives, without requiring the user to repeat the question.

These patterns require the model to have a sophisticated understanding of time and the ability to distinguish between relevant and irrelevant visual cues over long horizons.

## The Streaming Data Engine and Balanced Learning

Training a model to be "always-on" requires a specific type of dataset that does not exist in traditional computer vision benchmarks. AURA utilizes a Coarse-to-Fine Streaming Data Engine to generate high-quality training samples. This five-stage pipeline involves collecting diverse videos, using automated Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) to synthesize complex QA pairs with precise timestamps, and refining those questions for difficulty and phrasing diversity.

![The five-stage coarse-to-fine streaming data generation pipeline.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04184v1/x4.png)

A key challenge in training unified streaming models is the "silence/speech imbalance." In a typical stream, the model should be silent most of the time. If trained with standard methods, the model might learn to over-predict the `<|silent|>` token to minimize its loss, effectively "becoming mute." 

AURA addresses this with a Silent-Speech Balanced Loss. During training, the system applies a weighting strategy:
1.  Loss is only calculated for the current response and the associated silent tokens.
2.  Non-silent response tokens are given a full weight of 1.
3.  Silent message tokens are down-weighted based on their frequency using the formula $\text{Weight}_{\text{silent}} = 1/N_{\text{silent}}$.

This prevents the model from being overwhelmed by the high frequency of silence and ensures it remains responsive when an actual answer is needed.

## Real-Time System Performance and Results

AURA is built upon a $2 \text{ FPS}$ (frames per second) processing rate. To make the model practical for real-world use, the researchers integrated it into a full system including Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and Text-to-Speech (TTS) modules.

![The end-to-end real-time streaming inference framework integrating ASR, AURA, and TTS.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04184v1/x5.png)

In terms of latency, AURA achieves an end-to-end response time of approximately 312.2 ms. This means that from the moment a user finishes speaking, it takes less than a third of a second for the AI to start its spoken response. This speed is achieved through several optimizations:
*   **KV-Cache Reuse:** The system avoids recomputing visual information by caching key-value (KV) states.
*   **Context Truncation Optimization:** Instead of clearing the cache constantly, AURA allows the video window to grow slightly by $N'$ chunks before truncating. When the context reaches $N+N'$, it removes the oldest $N'$ chunks and recomputes only the remaining $N$. This reduces computational spikes.
*   **Asynchronous Processing:** ASR, the core AURA model, and TTS operate on separate threads to minimize bottlenecks.

## Benchmark Performance and Significance

The performance of AURA was evaluated on several specialized streaming benchmarks, including StreamingBench, OVO-Bench, and OmniMMI. On StreamingBench, AURA achieved an accuracy of 73.1%, outperforming proprietary models like Gemini-1.5-Pro by 6.0% and leading open-source alternatives by over 10%. It showed particular strength in "Backward Tracing" (remembering past events) and "Real-Time Visual Perception."

![Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) and cache token analysis showing the benefits of AURA's sliding window compared to unbounded context.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04184v1/x8.png)

As shown in the performance analysis, models without a sliding window experience a linear increase in Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) as the session continues, eventually becoming too slow for real-time interaction. AURA maintains a consistent, low TTFT by keeping the number of computed tokens bounded.

The significance of this work lies in its move toward truly interactive AI. By combining efficient context management with a training strategy that balances silence and speech, AURA provides a blueprint for next-generation assistants that can "see" the world in real-time. It preserves the high-level reasoning of large offline models while introducing the temporal stability required for continuous operation. The release of the AURA model and its inference framework offers a robust foundation for future research in autonomous systems and proactive human-AI collaboration.

keyInsights: A dual sliding-window context management strategy is effective for handling unbounded video and text inputs in streaming VideoLLMs, balancing the retention of critical history with the need for bounded context and real-time inference.

The Silent-Speech Balanced Loss objective is crucial for training streaming VideoLLMs to selectively produce responses or remain silent, while also mitigating issues like over-generating silent tokens or hallucinations arising from dynamic context truncation.

Co-designing the data generation pipeline, training objectives, and an optimized inference framework is essential for developing robust, end-to-end streaming VideoLLMs capable of diverse interaction modes (real-time, proactive, multi-response) over extended durations.
originalProblem: Existing Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) are primarily designed for offline video processing, which limits their application in real-time scenarios requiring immediate understanding and timely responses.

Current streaming VideoLLMs face limitations: decoupled architectures suffer from inconsistent contextual states, while unified architectures often lack robustness for long-duration interactions, are limited to captioning, or struggle with memory and performance degradation due to unbounded context.

Efficiently managing the continuously growing video and textual interaction history in a real-time streaming environment without exceeding the limited context window of LLMs poses a significant challenge.
results: AURA demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on StreamingBench (73.1% accuracy, 10.4% higher than MiniCPM-o-4.5), OVO-Bench (65.3% accuracy, 4.2% higher than ViSpeak), and OmniMMI (25.4% accuracy), outperforming both open-source and proprietary models like Gemini-1.5-Pro on various streaming benchmarks.

The integrated ASR-AURA-TTS system achieved a low end-to-end latency of approximately 312.2 ms from user speech input to the first spoken response, with the AURA model generating responses at around 137 tokens/s.

Streaming-oriented training, despite its specialized nature, largely preserved offline video understanding capabilities, showing only modest performance drops on traditional offline benchmarks compared to the base model.
solution: AURA proposes an end-to-end unified streaming visual interaction framework, co-designed across data, algorithms, and systems, to achieve stability, broad capabilities, and long-term endurance in real-time video understanding.

It employs an Interactive Video Stream Context Management system, featuring a dual sliding-window strategy for recent video content and QA interaction history, to effectively manage unbounded multimodal inputs.

A Coarse-to-Fine Streaming Data Engine generates diverse training data for three types of streaming QA interactions: Real-Time, Proactive, and Multi-Response, enabling nuanced conversational behaviors. A Silent-Speech Balanced Loss objective is introduced to handle the imbalance between silent and spoken responses and mitigate hallucination.
summary.summary: Researchers from Huawei Research and CUHK MMLab introduced AURA, an end-to-end streaming VideoLLM framework capable of continuously processing live video, making autonomous decisions on when to respond, and managing unbounded multimodal context. This framework achieved superior performance on streaming video understanding benchmarks, outperforming existing open-source and proprietary models, and demonstrated an end-to-end latency of approximately 312.2 ms for interactive scenarios.

title: AURA: Always-On Understanding and Real-Time Assistance via Video Streams
